h1Title: Post-Stroke Rehabilitation: Recovery, Therapy, and Prognosis
heroOverview: Post-stroke rehabilitation is the coordinated, multidisciplinary process of restoring function and independence after the brain injury caused by a stroke event. Approximately 12.2 million strokes occur worldwide each year, leaving 5 million survivors with permanent disability. Neurological recovery is greatest in the first 3 months after stroke — the period of peak brain neuroplasticity — making early, intensive rehabilitation the single most important determinant of long-term functional outcome. Stroke rehabilitation encompasses physiotherapy for limb weakness, speech-language pathology for aphasia and swallowing, occupational therapy for daily living skills, and psychological support for depression. Robot-assisted therapy, constraint-induced movement therapy, and non-invasive brain stimulation have expanded the recovery toolkit over the past two decades.

keyStats.prevalence: 12.2 million new strokes annually worldwide (GBD 2019); approximately 800,000 per year in the US alone; 5 million left with permanent disability
keyStats.demographics: Risk doubles each decade after age 55; men have higher incidence but women have higher lifetime risk; Black Americans have 2× higher stroke incidence than white Americans
keyStats.avgAge: Median stroke age 68–74 in high-income countries; rising incidence in adults aged 18–49 (now approximately 10% of all strokes)
keyStats.globalCases: Stroke is the leading cause of adult disability globally; 80 million people are living with stroke worldwide (GBD 2019); approximately 50% require some rehabilitation

definition: Post-stroke rehabilitation (ICD-10: Z87.39 — history of stroke, with associated rehabilitation codes Z50.1–Z50.9 for therapy type) describes the structured medical and therapeutic intervention applied after an acute ischaemic or haemorrhagic stroke to maximize neurological recovery, functional independence, and quality of life. The neurological basis for rehabilitation is cortical and subcortical neuroplasticity — the capacity of surviving neurons to reorganize synaptic connections, recruit peri-infarct cortical areas, and establish compensatory motor pathways. Animal models and human neuroimaging studies confirm that repetitive task-specific practice drives axonal sprouting, dendritic arborisation, and myelination in surviving brain tissue, with the greatest capacity for reorganization in the first 3 months. The American Heart Association/American Stroke Association 2021 post-stroke rehabilitation guidelines and the Royal College of Physicians 2023 National Clinical Guideline for Stroke both recommend early rehabilitation: begin passive mobilization within 24–48 hours of stroke onset for stable patients, progress to active rehabilitation within 72 hours if medically safe, and aim for 45 minutes or more of therapy per rehabilitation day. Physical medicine and rehabilitation specialists (physiatrists) coordinate the multidisciplinary team, which includes physiotherapists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, neuropsychologists, orthotists, and social workers.

typesClassification.description: Dedicated ward with daily coordinated multidisciplinary therapy; reduces death and dependency by 20% compared with general medical ward care (Cochrane 2020 meta-analysis); recommended for all patients with residual neurological deficit after acute management.

Community-based intensive therapy for patients who are medically stable but have mild-to-moderate disability; ESD with a specialist team achieves equivalent outcomes to continued inpatient care while enabling earlier return home. Cochrane 2017 review: reduces death and dependency (OR 0.80).

Ambulatory therapy for patients living at home with residual deficits; typically 3–5 sessions per week across a 12–24 week programme. Includes physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and psychology as needed.

The less-affected limb is restrained in a mitt for up to 90% of waking hours while the paretic limb practices intensive task-specific exercises (6 hours daily for 2 weeks). AHA/ASA 2021 Guidelines strongly recommend for mild-to-moderate upper-limb paresis.

End-effector or exoskeleton robots assist or resist limb movement through repetitive task-specific trajectories. Provides high-repetition practice (hundreds of movements per session) not achievable with manual therapy alone. Evidence strongest for upper-limb recovery; lower-limb exoskeletons accelerate gait relearning.

primarySymptoms: Contralateral limb weakness (hemiparesis or hemiplegia) affecting the face, arm, and leg to varying degrees — present in 80–85% of acute strokes; the primary target of physiotherapy.

Spasticity — velocity-dependent increase in muscle tone, most prominent in the shoulder adductors, elbow flexors, wrist and finger flexors, hip adductors, and ankle plantarflexors — developing in 25–43% of patients within 3 months.

Aphasia (language disorder) — impaired speaking, comprehension, reading, or writing from dominant hemisphere strokes; affects 30–40% of stroke survivors and is the primary target of speech-language pathology.
Dysphagia (swallowing disorder) — present in 37–78% of acute stroke patients; requires early screening and specialist assessment to prevent aspiration pneumonia.
Hemineglect — failure to respond to or acknowledge stimuli on the affected side; most common after right hemisphere strokes; affects functional recovery and rehabilitation engagement.
Post-stroke depression — affects 30–35% of survivors in the first year; significantly impairs rehabilitation participation and long-term functional recovery.
Cognitive impairment — executive dysfunction, memory deficits, and processing-speed slowing affect 35–40% of post-stroke patients; impedes therapy learning and increases carer burden.
Shoulder pain — occurs in 20–40% of stroke patients with upper-limb paresis; causes include subluxation, spasticity, brachial plexus traction, and complex regional pain syndrome.

Gait disorder — broad-based, circumducting, or scissoring gait patterns from lower-limb spasticity and weakness; the focus of treadmill, functional electrical stimulation, and robot-assisted gait training.
earlyWarningSigns: Dysphagia identified on initial swallowing screen — silent aspiration in an acute stroke patient without dysphagia management substantially increases 30-day pneumonia risk.
Early spasticity in wrist and finger flexors within the first 2 weeks — prompt positioning, splinting, and physiotherapy prevents permanent contracture.

Signs of post-stroke depression (low mood, tearfulness, withdrawal from therapy) in the first 2–4 weeks — early psychological intervention and antidepressant treatment improve rehabilitation adherence.
emergencySigns: Acute neurological deterioration during rehabilitation (sudden new weakness, speech change, altered consciousness) — possible haemorrhagic transformation, re-stroke, or seizure requiring urgent re-imaging and neurology review.
Signs of deep vein thrombosis (unilateral leg swelling, erythema, warmth) in a paretic immobile limb — urgent Doppler ultrasound and anticoagulation decision required.

Unexplained fever, productive cough, or oxygen desaturation in a dysphagic stroke patient — aspiration pneumonia is the leading post-stroke medical complication; prompt antibiotic therapy is critical.

causes.cause: Focal brain infarction (ischaemic stroke) causing cortical and subcortical neuronal death
causes.description: Occlusion of a cerebral artery deprives neurons of glucose and oxygen. Within minutes, irreversible infarction develops in the ischaemic core; surrounding penumbra neurons survive but become dysfunctional. Thrombolysis and thrombectomy salvage the penumbra; post-stroke rehabilitation addresses the functional consequences of infarct volume and location.

causes.cause: Intracerebral haemorrhage causing mass effect and secondary injury
causes.description: Bleeding into brain parenchyma produces haematoma, surrounding oedema, and cytotoxic injury. Haemorrhagic strokes are associated with faster early recovery in some functional domains (peri-haematoma oedema resolves) but slower return of fine motor function and higher rates of spasticity.

causes.cause: Subarachnoid haemorrhage with secondary neurological injury
causes.description: Aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage causes diffuse cortical injury and cerebral vasospasm, leading to delayed ischaemic deficits 4–14 days after the bleed. Post-SAH rehabilitation addresses cognitive, fatigue, and emotional deficits alongside focal neurological impairments.

causes.cause: Diaschisis — remote neuronal dysfunction beyond the infarct
causes.description: Neural circuits connected to the infarct zone temporarily shut down (diaschisis) even in structurally intact tissue. Recovery of diaschisis is a major mechanism of early spontaneous neurological improvement and explains why many patients improve significantly in the first weeks even without formal therapy.

Rehabilitation exploits activity-dependent neuroplasticity: repetitive task-specific practice drives synaptogenesis, axonal sprouting, and recruitment of alternative cortical representations for lost functions. Higher-intensity, task-specific practice produces more robust reorganization than low-intensity or passive approaches.

riskFactors.description: Infarct volume on DWI-MRI is the strongest imaging predictor of motor and cognitive outcome; infarcts above 15 mL in the motor cortex and internal capsule carry substantially lower recovery rates.
Initial stroke severity measured by the NIHSS is the best clinical predictor of functional outcome at 3 months; patients with NIHSS above 25 rarely achieve independent ambulation.

Older patients have reduced neuroplastic capacity, more comorbidities, and higher rates of cognitive impairment that limit rehabilitation learning. Nevertheless, age alone should not exclude intensive rehabilitation.

Depression reduces rehabilitation engagement by 40–60%; treatment with antidepressants (SSRIs) improves motor recovery independent of mood effects in some RCTs. Cognitive impairment limits motor learning but does not preclude benefit from rehabilitation.
Global aphasia (loss of all language modalities) reduces rehabilitation learning efficiency and increases carer burden; early speech therapy and communication aids are essential.

Each day of delay in beginning active rehabilitation within the first week reduces functional independence at 90 days. AHA/ASA recommends active therapy within 72 hours of stroke for medically stable patients.

riskFactors.factor: Medical comorbidities (atrial fibrillation, heart failure, diabetes)
riskFactors.description: Medical complications — pneumonia, urinary infection, decompensated heart failure — interrupt rehabilitation and independently worsen outcome. Active comorbidity management during rehabilitation is essential.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 55 and above: risk doubles each decade; 70% of all strokes occur in people over 65 in high-income countries.
Men: incidence 1.3–1.5× higher than women at age 45–80; women have higher lifetime risk due to greater longevity.
Black and Hispanic Americans: 2× higher stroke incidence than white Americans; younger age at first stroke; more lacunar infarcts from hypertension.

Low-income countries carry 80% of global stroke burden; limited rehabilitation infrastructure means most survivors receive no formal rehabilitation — leaving disability rates 50–60% higher than in high-income settings.
diagnosisOverview: Rehabilitation assessment follows acute stroke management and begins with standardized neurological and functional measures. The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) quantifies acute deficit severity across 15 items. The modified Rankin Scale (mRS) grades functional independence at 0–6; disability is mRS 3–5. The Fugl-Meyer Assessment is the gold standard for quantifying upper-limb motor impairment (0–66 points) and guides goal setting and treatment selection. Functional ambulation status is assessed by the Functional Ambulation Category (FAC) and timed-up-and-go test. Dysphagia is screened with the gugging swallowing screen (GUSS) or water swallow test within the first 4 hours of admission; failure triggers a formal speech-language pathology assessment and instrumental dysphagia evaluation (videofluoroscopy or FEES). Cognitive screening with the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) identifies post-stroke cognitive impairment in over 35% of patients. Depression is screened using the PHQ-9 or the Post-Stroke Depression Rating Scale. Upper-limb and lower-limb spasticity is graded by the Modified Ashworth Scale. Neuroimaging (CT/MRI) localizes the infarct and guides rehabilitation prognosis: hand-knob motor cortex and internal capsule involvement predict upper-limb outcome; posterior limb of internal capsule integrity on DWI is the strongest structural predictor of arm recovery.

diagnosticTests.test: NIHSS (National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardized 15-item neurological examination quantifying stroke severity from 0 (normal) to 42 (most severe); guides acute management and rehabilitation prognosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A trained clinician assesses level of consciousness, gaze, facial droop, arm and leg strength, ataxia, sensory loss, language, dysarthria, and extinction. Takes 7–10 minutes. No equipment other than a penlight required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades global disability from 0 (no symptoms) to 6 (death); the primary outcome measure in stroke trials and rehabilitation benchmarking
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-minute structured interview and observation of functional ability. Assessed at discharge, 30 days, and 90 days post-stroke.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies motor impairment severity in the paretic limb; guides goal-setting for CIMT, robotics, and conventional therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A physiotherapist examines reflexes, synergy patterns, and isolated joint movements across 66 upper-limb and 34 lower-limb items. Takes 30–45 minutes. Scored 0–2 per item.

diagnosticTests.test: Gugging Swallowing Screen (GUSS) or bedside swallow assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies dysphagia and aspiration risk within hours of stroke to guide nil-by-mouth decisions and feeding route
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The assessor tests semisolid, liquid, and solid food swallowing while monitoring for coughing, voice change, and desaturation. Takes 15–20 minutes. Performed by a nurse or speech therapist.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with DWI and FLAIR sequences
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms infarct location, volume, and involvement of motor pathways; internal capsule DWI integrity predicts arm recovery probability
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30–40 minutes in the scanner. DWI confirms acute ischaemia within minutes of onset. FLAIR shows chronic ischaemia and white matter disease. No contrast needed for most stroke evaluations.

diagnosticTests.purpose: 10-minute bedside screening for post-stroke cognitive impairment across memory, attention, executive function, and visuospatial domains
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient completes drawing, naming, counting, memory, and orientation tasks. Score below 26/30 suggests cognitive impairment requiring neuropsychological referral.

treatmentOverview: Stroke rehabilitation is guided by the AHA/ASA 2021 Guidelines for Adult Stroke Rehabilitation and Recovery and the Royal College of Physicians 2023 National Clinical Guideline for Stroke. The core principle is task-specific, high-intensity, early practice. Key evidence-based components: early mobilization (upright positioning and gentle passive range-of-motion within 24–48 hours for stable patients); inpatient stroke rehabilitation unit (reduces death and dependency by 20% vs. general ward — Cochrane); physiotherapy (task-specific motor training, constraint-induced movement therapy, treadmill gait training with harness); occupational therapy (ADL retraining, home modification, upper-limb function); speech-language therapy (aphasia therapy, dysphagia management); spasticity management (stretching, splinting, botulinum toxin, baclofen); and psychological support (CBT, antidepressants for post-stroke depression). Constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT) achieves significantly better upper-limb function than usual care at 2 years in the EXCITE trial (Wolf et al. JAMA 2006). Robot-assisted therapy (end-effector devices, exoskeletons) provides hundreds of repetitions per session and achieves comparable upper-limb outcomes to intensive conventional therapy with less therapist burden; it is recommended as an adjunct in AHA/ASA 2021 Guidelines. Functional electrical stimulation (FES) to the dorsiflexor muscles reduces foot drop and improves gait speed. Non-invasive brain stimulation (rTMS, tDCS) has emerging but not yet guideline-endorsed evidence for aphasia and motor recovery. Longer-duration and higher-intensity therapy consistently produces better outcomes; the VECTORS trial found that very early intensive therapy (before 24 hours) may temporarily worsen outcomes in some patients — current consensus recommends beginning within 24–72 hours for stable patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Early mobilization protocol (sitting, standing, transfer within 24–72 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured early mobilization by rehabilitation nurses and physiotherapists; aims for out-of-bed sitting within 24 hours for stable patients. Prevents deep vein thrombosis, pneumonia, pressure sores, and orthostatic hypotension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Early mobilization within 24 hours reduces hospital-acquired complications by 30–40%; very early intensive mobilization before 24 hours has neutral to slightly negative effect on 3-month outcome per AVERT trial 2015

medicalTreatments.name: Task-specific physiotherapy and motor training
medicalTreatments.description: Repetitive practice of functional tasks — reaching, grasping, walking, transfer, balance — targeted at the patient's specific motor deficit. Intensity of at least 45 minutes per discipline per rehabilitation day is the AHA/ASA minimum standard.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dose-response relationship: each additional hour of task-specific practice per week improves Fugl-Meyer score by 1.2–2.0 points over 12 weeks per meta-analysis

medicalTreatments.name: Constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT)
medicalTreatments.description: Affected hand mitt restraint of the unaffected limb combined with 6 hours daily intensive affected-arm practice for 2 weeks. Best evidence for patients with at least 10° active wrist extension and 10° finger extension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: EXCITE RCT (Wolf et al. JAMA 2006): sustained Arm Motor Activity Test improvement vs. usual care at 2 years; effect size 0.5–0.8

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin type A (50–200 units per muscle group) for spasticity
medicalTreatments.description: Injected into spastic muscles (biceps, flexor carpi radialis, gastrocnemius, tibialis posterior) to reduce tone and improve passive range. Combined with physiotherapy stretching for 8–12 weeks post-injection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modified Ashworth Scale reduction of 1–2 points at 4–12 weeks in upper and lower limb; significant improvement in passive range of motion and caregiver burden

medicalTreatments.name: SSRI antidepressants (fluoxetine 20 mg daily) for post-stroke depression
medicalTreatments.description: Treat post-stroke depression, which affects 30–35% of survivors and substantially impairs rehabilitation engagement. Fluoxetine has evidence for mild motor-recovery benefit independent of antidepressant effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SSRIs reduce post-stroke depression prevalence from 35% to 20% at 3–6 months; FLAME trial found fluoxetine improved Fugl-Meyer motor score by 8 points vs. placebo at 3 months

medicalTreatments.name: Speech-language pathology (aphasia therapy, dysphagia management)
medicalTreatments.description: Intensive evidence-based aphasia therapy including constraint-induced aphasia therapy (CIAT), semantic feature analysis, and conversation partner training. Dysphagia therapy includes compensatory strategies, oral-motor exercises, and dietary modification.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Intensive aphasia therapy (5 hours daily) improves communication outcomes significantly vs. low-intensity; modified diet reduces aspiration pneumonia incidence by 50%

surgicalOptions.name: Neurosurgical intervention for haemorrhagic transformation or oedema
surgicalOptions.description: Hemicraniectomy for malignant middle-cerebral-artery infarction reduces mortality by 50% in selected patients under 60; haematoma evacuation may be performed for selected intracerebral haemorrhages. These are acute neurosurgical decisions, not rehabilitation interventions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: DESTINY and HAMLET trials: hemicraniectomy reduces death from 80% to 40% in malignant MCA infarction; good functional outcome (mRS 3) in 30–40% at 12 months

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture (adjunct)
alternativeTreatments.description: A Cochrane review (2016; 33 RCTs, 3,946 patients) found acupuncture during subacute rehabilitation improved neurological deficit scores and functional independence scores vs. control at 3–6 months, though evidence quality was low-to-moderate.

alternativeTreatments.name: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-invasive cortical stimulation using a magnetic coil; inhibitory 1 Hz rTMS over the intact hemisphere or excitatory 10 Hz rTMS over the affected hemisphere improves motor and language outcomes in small-to-medium RCTs; AHA/ASA 2021 rates evidence Class IIb.

whySeeSpecialist: A physiatrist (Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation specialist) should lead stroke rehabilitation from day 3–5 post-stroke, directing the multidisciplinary team, prescribing spasticity management, and setting functional goals. Neurologists and internists manage acute management and secondary prevention while physiatrists own the rehabilitation trajectory.
doctorSelectionGuide: Select a physiatrist with stroke rehabilitation subspecialty experience — ideally affiliated with a Joint Commission Certified Stroke Rehabilitation program or equivalent national certification. For CIMT or robot-assisted therapy, choose a unit with dedicated devices and trained therapists conducting at least 50 stroke rehabilitation admissions per year.
hospitalCriteria: Dedicated stroke rehabilitation unit or ward with coordinated multidisciplinary team
Robot-assisted therapy devices for upper and lower limb (Armeo, Lokomat, or equivalent)
FEES or videofluoroscopy for instrumental dysphagia assessment
Neuropsychology service for cognitive and psychological assessment
keyFacilities: Comprehensive stroke centers with dedicated rehabilitation units
Inpatient rehabilitation hospitals with stroke specialization
Community stroke rehabilitation teams (early supported discharge programs)
Outpatient neurological physiotherapy and occupational therapy services
preventionStrategies: Begin passive ROM exercises within 24–48 hours of stroke in medically stable patients — prevents contracture, DVT, and shoulder pain while conditioning the neural substrate for active recovery.
Start swallowing screening on admission — early identification of dysphagia and NGT feeding when needed prevents aspiration pneumonia, the leading cause of death in the first 30 days.
Optimise blood pressure, anticoagulation, and antiplatelet therapy for secondary stroke prevention throughout rehabilitation — recurrent stroke occurs in 5–10% within 90 days.
Screen for depression with PHQ-9 at 2 weeks and 3 months post-stroke; commence SSRI early for positive screens to preserve rehabilitation engagement.
Engage family and carers in training from day 1 — caregiver-mediated exercises at least 20 minutes daily approximately double the dose of task-specific practice achieved in hospital settings.
lifestyleModifications: Perform daily home exercise program as prescribed — at least 20–30 minutes of task-specific arm and/or gait practice on all non-therapy days.

Resume a Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and oily fish to reduce recurrent stroke risk — Mediterranean diet reduces cardiovascular events by 25–30% in secondary prevention.
Eliminate smoking if a smoker — smoking doubles recurrent stroke risk; cessation reduces risk to near-baseline within 2–5 years.
Limit alcohol to no more than 14 units per week — alcohol above moderate levels increases stroke recurrence and impairs neural recovery.
Manage blood pressure at home with a validated home monitor — target below 130/80 mmHg for most post-stroke patients per AHA/ASA guidelines.
Maintain social engagement and cognitive activity — social isolation and cognitive inactivity impair neuroplasticity and worsen post-stroke depression.

recommended: Mediterranean diet pattern (olive oil, vegetables, fish, legumes, whole grains) — reduces recurrent cardiovascular event risk by 25–30% vs. a Western diet in secondary prevention trials
Adequate protein (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day) to prevent sarcopenia and support muscle strengthening during physiotherapy
Omega-3 fatty acids (2 g EPA+DHA daily) — anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective; recommended in AHA secondary prevention guidelines
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation for post-stroke patients with osteoporosis risk from immobility and corticosteroid use
avoid: High-sodium foods (targets below 2,300 mg/day sodium) — excess sodium raises blood pressure and increases recurrent stroke risk
Trans fats and high saturated-fat foods — accelerate atherosclerosis and increase recurrent ischaemic stroke risk
Excessive alcohol — impairs neuroplasticity, raises blood pressure, and increases haemorrhagic stroke risk
High-glycemic index foods — poor glycemic control impairs post-stroke neurological recovery and increases recurrent ischaemic risk

exerciseGuidelines: During inpatient rehabilitation, 45 minutes minimum of physiotherapy per day is the AHA/ASA standard. After discharge, progress to 150 minutes moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week as tolerated — walking (with assistive device if needed), stationary cycling, and water therapy are safe starting points. Resistance training for paretic and compensating limbs is introduced at 4–8 weeks under therapist supervision. Cardiorespiratory fitness is substantially impaired after stroke — structured exercise programs improve fitness, depression, and recurrent stroke risk simultaneously.
dailyManagement: Take all secondary prevention medications (antiplatelet/anticoagulant, statin, antihypertensive) at the same time each day without missing doses.
Perform prescribed home exercise program for 20–30 minutes daily — note specific exercises, repetitions, and any assistive devices on the daily log sheet.
Use prescribed orthotics (ankle-foot orthosis for foot drop, wrist splint for flexor spasticity) during all weight-bearing activity; remove at rest for skin inspection.
Monitor blood pressure at home each morning and record results; contact the care team if systolic exceeds 160 mmHg on two consecutive readings.
Attend all outpatient therapy appointments; missed sessions should be rescheduled within 48 hours to maintain recovery momentum.
Report any new weakness, speech change, sudden headache, or visual disturbance immediately — these may indicate a transient ischaemic attack or recurrent stroke requiring emergency assessment.
prognosis: Post-stroke prognosis depends on stroke type, location, size, and time to treatment. Approximately 30–35% of ischaemic stroke patients achieve full independence (mRS 0–1) at 90 days. About 20–25% achieve good recovery (mRS 2) and 25–30% are moderately to severely dependent (mRS 3–5). Haemorrhagic stroke has higher acute mortality (35–50%) but survivors show comparable or better functional recovery than ischaemic stroke. The NIHSS score at 24 hours is the strongest predictor of 90-day outcome. Upper-limb recovery follows a predictable pattern: patients with any finger extension at 72 hours recover useful hand function in 70–80% of cases; those with no movement in the first 4 weeks recover useful hand function in approximately 25%. Aphasia improvement is lifelong but most rapid in the first 3–6 months. Post-stroke depression at 3 months doubles the probability of remaining dependent at 12 months. With optimal rehabilitation, a majority of patients make meaningful functional gains extending beyond 6 months from stroke onset.
recoveryTimeline: Most spontaneous neurological recovery occurs in the first 3 months. Upper-limb recovery: 50% of potential gains by 6 weeks, 85–90% by 6 months. Gait recovery: most ambulatory patients walk independently by 3–6 months. Language recovery continues for 1–2 years. Late recovery (months 6–24) continues with targeted therapy — the brain retains neuroplastic capacity beyond 6 months contrary to historical belief.
complications: Post-stroke spasticity — develops in 25–43% of patients by 3 months; if untreated, causes contracture, pain, and hygiene difficulty; managed with physiotherapy, splinting, and botulinum toxin.
Post-stroke depression — 30–35% at 12 months; nearly doubles the risk of functional dependence at 12 months; screen with PHQ-9 and treat with SSRIs + CBT.
Aspiration pneumonia — the leading cause of post-stroke death in the first 30 days; prevented by early dysphagia screening and dietary modification.
Deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — immobility and hemiplegia increase DVT risk 10× in the first 30 days; managed with compression stockings and anticoagulation.

Falls and hip fracture — patients with hemiparesis and balance disorder have a 3× higher fall rate than age-matched controls; hip protectors, bed/chair alarms, and supervised mobility reduce fracture risk.

Shoulder-hand syndrome (complex regional pain syndrome type 1) — painful shoulder and hand oedema in 2–5% of post-stroke patients with upper-limb paresis; managed with corticosteroids, TENS, and mirror visual feedback therapy.

supportResources.name: American Stroke Association — Life After Stroke
supportResources.description: AHA/ASA comprehensive guide to stroke recovery, caregiver support, aphasia resources, and community rehabilitation programs.

supportResources.name: Stroke Association UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing helpline support, rehabilitation resource guides, and peer-support networks for stroke survivors and carers.

supportResources.name: National Aphasia Association
supportResources.description: Dedicated resource for post-stroke aphasia with communication aids, speech therapy directories, and support group locator.

confusedWithConditions.name: Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) Rehabilitation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TBI rehabilitation addresses diffuse axonal injury from mechanical trauma, with frontal lobe dysfunction, behavioral changes, and post-concussion syndrome as dominant features. Post-stroke rehabilitation targets focal cortical or subcortical infarcts producing hemiparesis, aphasia, or hemispatial neglect. Neuroimaging clearly distinguishes the two, and rehabilitation goals differ by deficit profile.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spinal Cord Injury Rehabilitation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spinal cord injury produces bilateral motor and sensory deficits below the level of the lesion, with preserved cognitive and language function. Post-stroke rehabilitation typically targets unilateral hemiparesis and cortical deficits (aphasia, neglect, cognitive impairment). Management differs substantially, including use of CIMT in stroke vs. powered wheelchair and bladder programs in SCI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Central Post-Stroke Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Central post-stroke pain (Dejerine-Roussy syndrome) is a specific complication of thalamic or spinothalamic tract infarction producing allodynia and burning pain in a hemibody distribution. It is a complication of stroke rather than a competing diagnosis, but it complicates rehabilitation by limiting therapy participation and requires specific pharmacological management with amitriptyline or lamotrigine.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: The acute event that precedes and necessitates post-stroke rehabilitation; stroke type, location, and severity are the primary determinants of rehabilitation prognosis.

relatedConditions.name: TBI Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares neuroplasticity-based rehabilitation principles with post-stroke rehab; physiatrists manage both conditions using overlapping multimodal therapy frameworks.

relatedConditions.name: Spinal Cord Injury Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are managed by physiatrists in dedicated neurorehabilitation units; functional electrical stimulation, robotics, and exercise-based neuroplasticity approaches are shared across both conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Myofascial Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Muscle imbalance, spasticity, and altered load-bearing after stroke produce secondary myofascial trigger points that are addressed in comprehensive stroke rehabilitation.

faqs.question: When should stroke rehabilitation start?
faqs.answer: AHA/ASA and RCP guidelines recommend beginning passive mobilization and positioning within 24–48 hours of stroke onset in medically stable patients. Active therapy with a physiotherapist and occupational therapist should begin within 72 hours. Earlier rehabilitation is associated with better functional outcomes at 3 months.

faqs.question: How long does stroke recovery take?
faqs.answer: Most neurological recovery occurs in the first 3 months, with the greatest gains in the first 4–6 weeks. Meaningful recovery continues up to 6–12 months, and motor, language, and cognitive improvements can occur for 2 or more years with targeted therapy. Most ambulatory patients achieve independent walking within 3–6 months.

faqs.question: What is constraint-induced movement therapy for stroke?
faqs.answer: CIMT restrains the unaffected arm in a padded mitt for most of the waking day while intensively practising tasks with the paretic arm for 6 hours daily over 2 weeks. The EXCITE trial showed significantly better arm function than usual care at 2 years. It is suitable for patients with at least 10 degrees of active wrist and finger extension.

faqs.question: Can arm function return after a stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. Patients with any active finger or wrist extension within 72 hours of stroke recover useful arm function in 70–80% of cases. Even patients with no early movement can recover some function with intensive therapy. Upper-limb recovery continues with practice for up to 2 years post-stroke.

faqs.question: What therapies are used in stroke rehabilitation?
faqs.answer: Stroke rehabilitation uses physiotherapy for limb weakness and gait, occupational therapy for daily living and arm function, speech-language pathology for aphasia and dysphagia, neuropsychology for cognitive impairment, and psychology for depression. Botulinum toxin treats spasticity. Robot-assisted therapy and CIMT provide high-intensity supplementary motor practice.

faqs.question: What is spasticity after stroke and how is it treated?
faqs.answer: Spasticity is a velocity-dependent increase in muscle tone from upper motor neuron damage, affecting 25–43% of stroke survivors by 3 months. It causes stiffness, pain, and contracture. Treatment includes stretching, splinting, physiotherapy, and botulinum toxin injections into spastic muscle groups, reducing tone for 12–16 weeks per injection.

faqs.question: Does depression affect stroke recovery?
faqs.answer: Yes, substantially. Post-stroke depression affects 30–35% of survivors and approximately doubles the risk of remaining dependent at 12 months by reducing therapy engagement and neuroplasticity. SSRIs (fluoxetine 20 mg) reduce depression rates and may independently improve motor recovery. CBT improves quality of life alongside medication.

faqs.question: What is aphasia and how is it treated after stroke?
faqs.answer: Aphasia is a language disorder affecting speaking, comprehension, reading, or writing, resulting from damage to language cortex (usually left hemisphere). It affects 30–40% of stroke survivors. Intensive speech-language therapy — at least 5 hours weekly in the acute phase — produces significantly better communication outcomes than low-intensity therapy.

faqs.question: Can robots be used to help stroke rehabilitation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Robot-assisted therapy devices guide and assist paretic limb movement through hundreds of repetitions per session — far more than possible with manual therapy alone. Arm robots (Armeo, AMADEO) and gait exoskeletons (Lokomat) improve motor function comparably to intensive conventional therapy. AHA/ASA 2021 Guidelines rate robot-assisted therapy as a reasonable adjunct.

faqs.question: What is the best exercise after a stroke?
faqs.answer: The most effective exercises are task-specific — practising actual functional activities like reaching, grasping, standing from a chair, and walking rather than generic exercises. For cardiovascular health, 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity weekly is recommended. Resistance training for paretic and compensating muscles is added after 4–8 weeks.

faqs.question: What is a stroke rehabilitation unit?
faqs.answer: A dedicated inpatient ward staffed by a coordinated team of physiotherapists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, nurses, and rehabilitation physicians. Cochrane meta-analysis (2020) shows stroke units reduce death and dependency by 20% compared with general medical wards. All stroke patients with residual deficit should be admitted to a stroke unit.

faqs.question: Can walking be recovered after stroke?
faqs.answer: The majority of stroke survivors who retain some lower-limb movement in the first week recover the ability to walk by 6 months. Up to 85% of patients admitted to stroke rehabilitation units achieve independent or assisted walking. Treadmill training, functional electrical stimulation, and gait exoskeletons accelerate gait recovery.

faqs.question: What is foot drop after stroke?
faqs.answer: Foot drop is weakness of ankle dorsiflexion from peroneal nerve or upper motor neuron damage, causing toe-catching and falls during the swing phase of gait. It affects 20–30% of stroke survivors with lower-limb paresis. An ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) provides immediate correction; functional electrical stimulation to the peroneal nerve improves long-term strength.

faqs.question: How much therapy should a stroke patient receive per day?
faqs.answer: AHA/ASA and RCP guidelines recommend a minimum of 45 minutes of physiotherapy and 45 minutes of occupational therapy per rehabilitation day, with additional speech therapy as needed. High-intensity programs providing 3 or more hours of active therapy daily achieve significantly better motor outcomes than low-intensity programs.

faqs.question: Can stroke rehabilitation continue after 6 months?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recovery is not limited to the first 6 months, despite the historical belief that the recovery window closes. Multiple RCTs show significant motor, language, and functional improvements from rehabilitation initiated 6–24 months post-stroke. The brain retains neuroplastic capacity for years, and high-intensity task-specific practice drives measurable improvements at any stage.

metaDescription: Post-stroke rehabilitation: coordinated therapy restoring movement, speech, and independence. Recovery, CIMT, robotics, and spasticity management reviewed.
